lessons.title: std::inplace_vector
lessons.theory:
## Why `std::inplace_vector`?

`std::vector` is powerful, but it has one unavoidable trait: **it always allocates on the heap**. In many scenarios, this is unacceptable:

- **Embedded systems** where heap allocation is forbidden or expensive
- **Hot loops** where allocation latency is prohibitive
- **Stack-local buffers**: temporary arrays with a known maximum size
- **Real-time systems**: code where exceptions are disabled or blocking is forbidden

Before C++26, workarounds included `std::array<T, N>` (fixed size, not dynamic) or manual `T buf[N]; size_t n = 0;` tricks (no iterator support, no STL algorithms).

C++26 solves this with `std::inplace_vector<T, N>` (P0843R10).

---

## `std::inplace_vector<T, N>`

`inplace_vector<T, N>` is a vector stored **entirely inline** (on the stack or inside a parent object), with zero heap allocation:

- **Capacity fixed** at compile time: always equals `N`
- **Size is dynamic**: `size()` ranges from 0 to N
- Include via `#include <inplace_vector>`

```cpp
#include <inplace_vector>

std::inplace_vector<int, 4> v; // buffer for 4 ints, currently empty
v.push_back(1);
v.push_back(2);
// v.size() == 2, v.capacity() == 4
```

---

## `inplace_vector` vs `array` vs `vector`

| Property | `inplace_vector<T,N>` | `array<T,N>` | `vector<T>` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storage | Inline (stack/struct) | Inline (stack/struct) | Heap |
| Capacity | Fixed (`N`) | Fixed (`N`) | Dynamic |
| Size | Dynamic (0..N) | Always `N` | Dynamic |
| `push_back` | Yes (throws on overflow) | No | Yes (reallocates) |
| Allocations | Never | Never | Always |
| Non-std analog | — | — | `boost::container::small_vector` |

Key difference from `array`: `array` **always has size N**, all N elements are initialized. `inplace_vector` starts at size 0 and grows dynamically, just like `vector`.

---

## Construction

```cpp
std::inplace_vector<int, 6> a;              // empty, size=0
std::inplace_vector<int, 6> b = {1, 2, 3}; // from initializer_list, size=3
std::inplace_vector<int, 6> c(4, 0);       // four zeros
```

If an initializer_list has more than N elements, `std::bad_alloc` is thrown.

---

## Adding elements

### Throwing variants

- `push_back(v)` — appends an element; throws `std::bad_alloc` if `size() == N`
- `emplace_back(args...)` — constructs in-place; same semantics

```cpp
std::inplace_vector<int, 3> v;
v.push_back(1);   // OK
v.push_back(2);   // OK
v.push_back(3);   // OK
v.push_back(4);   // std::bad_alloc!
```

### Non-throwing variants: `try_push_back` and `try_emplace_back`

Return `std::optional<T&>`: a reference to the inserted element, or `std::nullopt` on overflow:

```cpp
std::inplace_vector<int, 2> v;
auto r1 = v.try_push_back(10); // r1.has_value() == true
auto r2 = v.try_push_back(20); // r2.has_value() == true
auto r3 = v.try_push_back(30); // r3.has_value() == false (overflow)
```

These variants are critical for embedded and real-time code where exceptions are disabled.

---

## Other operations

- `pop_back()` — removes the last element
- `resize(n)` / `resize(n, val)` — resizes; throws `std::bad_alloc` if `n > N`
- `clear()` — empties the container (size=0)
- `erase(it)`, `insert(it, v)` — standard operations

---

## Capacity and iterators

- `capacity()` — **always equals N** (compile-time constant)
- `max_size()` — also N
- `reserve(k)` — **throws `std::bad_alloc` if `k > N`**; this is not a silent no-op!
- `data()` — pointer is stable **forever** (reallocation is impossible)
- Iterators are contiguous (like `std::vector`)

---

## Trivial relocatability

If `T` is trivially relocatable, `inplace_vector<T, N>` is too. This matters for efficient move operations on the entire container.

---

## Typical use cases

- **Parser token buffers**: `inplace_vector<Token, 64>` with no allocations
- **SIMD batches**: `inplace_vector<float, 8>` for SIMD processing
- **Embedded systems**: strict memory budget
- **Hot paths**: local temporary collections with a known maximum size

examples.title: Basic usage
try_push_back: non-throwing insertion
Sorting and dynamic growth

exercises.prompt: What happens when you call `push_back` on a full `inplace_vector` (size == N)?
options: Reallocates to a larger heap buffer

exercises.explanation: `reserve(n)` on `inplace_vector` throws `std::bad_alloc` when `n > N` (here N=3 and 6>3). Unlike `std::vector`, it cannot grow its storage. The fix: don't call `reserve` at all (capacity is already fixed at N), or switch to `std::vector` if you need dynamic growth.

questions.prompt: What does `try_push_back` return when the `inplace_vector` is full?
options: A reference to the last element

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between `inplace_vector<T,N>` and `array<T,N>`?
options: inplace_vector stores elements on the heap
inplace_vector has a dynamic size ≤ N, while array always has size N
array supports push_back but inplace_vector does not

questions.prompt: What happens when `push_back` is called on a full `inplace_vector`?
options: The container reallocates on the heap

questions.prompt: What is `capacity()` always equal to for `inplace_vector<T, N>`?

challenge.title: Top-5 without heap allocation
challenge.description:
Write a function `top_k` that takes a range of integers and returns an `inplace_vector<int, 5>` containing the five largest values sorted in descending order, using no heap allocation. Handle the case where the range has fewer than 5 elements.

Hint: use a min-heap (`std::push_heap` / `std::pop_heap` with `std::greater<>`) to track the running top-5.

lessons.title: std::hive
lessons.theory:
**Note:** `std::hive` is proposed for C++26 (P0447) but has not yet been implemented by any compiler. Code examples follow the proposal specification.

---

## The Cache-vs-Stability Dilemma

C++ programmers are well acquainted with the fundamental trade-off between two standard containers:

- **`std::vector`** — the gold standard for cache-friendliness: elements reside in a contiguous memory block, so iteration is a sequential scan of cache lines. But any insertion in the middle shifts all subsequent elements, and buffer growth (reallocation) invalidates **every** iterator, pointer, and reference at once.

- **`std::list`** — guarantees stability: insertion and erasure anywhere leave neighboring elements and their iterators/pointers untouched. But every node is a separate heap object, so iteration is a chain of scattered memory accesses — "pointer-chasing" — and the CPU's prefetcher cannot predict the next address, leading to constant cache misses.

Both extremes are unacceptable for a whole class of real-world systems: game engines, particle systems, physics simulations, ECS (Entity-Component-System) architectures — all of these need **simultaneously** fast iteration and stable element addresses (so that stored pointers/references never go stale).

Before C++26 this was solved by hand: "slot maps", "pool allocators", third-party libraries. `std::hive` standardizes the pattern.

---

## `std::hive<T>` — Overview (P0447)

`std::hive` is a **sequence container** (not ordered by key) designed exactly for the scenario above:

- insertion anywhere — **O(1) amortized**
- erasure anywhere — **O(1)**
- **stable** pointers and references to live elements
- iteration remains reasonably cache-friendly

Two key mechanisms deliver all of these properties at once: **segmented storage** (blocks) and a **skipfield**.

---

## Internal Structure: Blocks and the Skipfield

Elements are not stored in a single contiguous buffer but in **fixed-size memory blocks** (segments). Each block has:

1. An array of slots for elements of type `T`.
2. A **skipfield** — a bit (or byte) array of the same length, where each entry marks whether its slot is occupied or a "hole".

On **insertion**: if a free slot exists (from a previous erasure) it is reused; otherwise the next free slot in the current block is taken, or a new block is allocated.

On **erasure**: nothing is physically shifted. The slot is marked as a hole in the skipfield. Its address does not change — therefore pointers to all remaining elements stay valid.

On **iteration**: the traversal algorithm reads the skipfield and "jumps over" holes without touching erased slots. Access within each block is still sequential, so cache behavior is good at the block granularity.

This mechanism explains the name: a "hive" is a cellular structure with gaps where each cell stays fixed in place.

---

## Key API

```cpp
std::hive<T> h;                  // default-construct (empty)

auto it = h.insert(value);       // insert, O(1) amortized
                                 // returns iterator to the inserted element
auto it = h.emplace(args...);    // insert with in-place construction

h.erase(it);                     // erase by iterator, O(1)
                                 // invalidates only it; all other iterators remain valid

h.size();                        // number of live elements
h.empty();                       // true if no elements
h.max_size();                    // theoretical maximum

// Iteration — holes are skipped automatically
for (auto& x : h) { ... }

h.sort();                        // member sort (std::sort does not apply)
```

**What `std::hive` does NOT provide:**

- `operator[]` and `at()` — no random access by index (holes between live elements mean numeric indices are not stable)
- `push_back` / `push_front` — use `insert` instead
- `std::sort(h.begin(), h.end())` — `hive` iterators do not satisfy the RandomAccessIterator requirement; use `h.sort()`

---

## Iterator Stability Guarantees

| Operation | Iterators to other elements |
|---|---|
| `insert` / `emplace` | **Remain valid** ✅ |
| `erase(it)` | Only `it` is invalidated; all others **remain valid** ✅ |

For comparison: `std::vector::push_back` invalidates **all** iterators when reallocation occurs; `insert` in the middle invalidates all iterators past the insertion point.

---

## Block Size Tuning

`std::hive` accepts an optional third template parameter `BlockSizeLimits` to control minimum and maximum block sizes:

```cpp
std::hive<int, std::allocator<int>, std::hive_limits<8, 512>> h;
```

Smaller blocks reduce memory overhead for sparse usage. Larger blocks improve cache behavior for dense usage. By default the implementation chooses sizes automatically.

---

## Comparison Table

| Property | `vector` | `list` | `deque` | `hive` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cache-friendly | ✅ full | ❌ | ✓ partial | ✓ per-block |
| Stable pointers/references | ❌ (on realloc) | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| O(1) insert anywhere | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| O(1) erase anywhere | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Random access by index | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Sort via `std::sort` | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ (`.sort()` only) |

---

## When to Use `std::hive`

- **Particle systems and game entities**: thousands of objects with frequent birth/death, need to iterate all live ones fast.
- **ECS component stores**: components must have stable addresses (other systems hold pointers to them).
- **Object pools** with arbitrary object lifetimes.
- Any scenario requiring: **fast iteration + stable references + frequent arbitrary-position insert/erase**.

examples.title: Basic insert and erase
Pointer stability through many insertions

options: An iterator to the inserted element
The index of the inserted element
exercises.explanation: `hive::insert` returns an iterator to the inserted element — unlike `std::set::insert` which returns a pair, and unlike `std::vector::push_back` which returns void.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following statements about `std::hive` is true?
options: Inserting an element invalidates all existing iterators
Erasing an element invalidates only the iterator to that element
std::hive guarantees strictly contiguous storage of all elements
exercises.explanation: This is the defining property of `std::hive`: `erase(it)` invalidates only `it`; all other iterators and pointers stay valid. Insertion also does not invalidate existing iterators. `operator[]` is absent, and storage is segmented (not contiguous).

exercises.prompt: Find the bug in the following code:
exercises.explanation: `std::sort` requires RandomAccessIterators, but `std::hive` iterators are not random-access. Use the member function `h.sort()` instead — it is part of the container's API for exactly this purpose, analogous to `std::list::sort()`.

exercises.prompt: In which scenario is `std::hive` a better fit than `std::vector`?
options: You need fast random access by index
You need stable pointers during frequent insertions and erasures at arbitrary positions
You need minimal per-element memory overhead
You need to sort the collection with std::sort
exercises.explanation: `std::hive` is designed precisely for scenarios where element addresses must remain stable across active insertions and erasures. `std::vector` fails here because reallocation invalidates all pointers. Random access, minimal overhead, and `std::sort` are not strengths of `hive`.

options: Elements have no stable numeric indices — there may be holes between live elements
The designers simply did not add the method

questions.prompt: What happens to iterators pointing to other elements when `h.erase(it)` is called?
options: Only iterator `it` becomes invalid; all others remain valid
Iterators in the same internal block are invalidated

questions.prompt: For which scenario is `std::hive` best suited?
options: Particle systems: thousands of objects with stable references and frequent insertions/erasures

lessons.title: std::text_encoding
lessons.theory:
## Motivation

Before C++26, C++ had no standard way to ask: "What encoding are narrow string literals in this translation unit?" Code that worked perfectly on Linux (UTF-8) could silently produce garbage on Windows (CP1252, CP1251) — or vice versa. Developers relied on `#ifdef _WIN32`, fragile string comparisons, or simply ignored the problem.

C++26 adds `std::text_encoding` (P1885R4) — a type that names a character encoding via the IANA charset registry and gives a checkable answer right in code.

## The class `std::text_encoding`

`std::text_encoding` is a lightweight wrapper over an encoding identifier from the IANA registry. It is not directly constructible by users — static factory functions are used instead.

The key to it is the `.mib()` method, which returns a value of the `std::text_encoding::id` enumeration. MIBenum (Management Information Base enumeration) is a numeric identifier from the IANA registry — stable and platform-independent.

## Key factory functions

`std::text_encoding::literal()` — the encoding of **narrow string literals** (`char`) in the current translation unit. Determined at compile time by compiler flags (e.g., `-fexec-charset=UTF-8` for GCC). This is the encoding in which bytes of strings like `"Hello, world"` are stored in your binary.

The standard also describes `std::text_encoding::environment()` — the encoding of the runtime environment (locale). On Linux/macOS it is almost always UTF-8; on Windows it may be CP1252, CP1251, etc. **Note:** On Compiler Explorer (sandbox environment), `environment()` crashes due to sandbox restrictions — use it only in real applications.

## The `std::text_encoding::id` enum

The `id` enumeration contains standard MIBenum values:

| Value | MIB | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `id::UTF8` | 106 | UTF-8 |
| `id::ISO_8859_1` | 4 | Latin-1 |
| `id::UTF_16` | 1015 | UTF-16 |
| `id::other` | 2 | Non-standard |
| `id::unknown` | 0 | Unknown |

The correct way to check for UTF-8:

```cpp
auto enc = std::text_encoding::literal();
bool is_utf8 = (enc.mib() == std::text_encoding::id::UTF8);
```

## Methods

- `.name()` → `const char*` — the IANA name of the encoding (`"UTF-8"`, `"ISO-8859-1"`, `"windows-1252"`)
- `.mib()` → `std::text_encoding::id` — the numeric MIBenum identifier
- `operator==` / `operator!=` — comparison of two `text_encoding` objects

## Practical use

This API is needed when code must know: "Can I treat `char*` as UTF-8?"

```cpp
// Anti-pattern — fragile:
if (std::string(enc.name()) == "UTF-8") { ... }

// Correct:
if (enc.mib() == std::text_encoding::id::UTF8) { ... }
```

The `enc.name()` string returns the canonical IANA name. UTF-8 has dozens of registered aliases (`"utf-8"`, `"UTF8"`, `"csUTF8"`), and string comparison only catches the canonical form. Comparing by MIB is the only reliable method.

examples.title: Querying the literal encoding
examples.description: Get the name and MIB number of the encoding used by the compiler for narrow string literals. On a UTF-8 platform (Linux, Compiler Explorer) we expect `UTF-8` and MIB 106.

examples.title: Checking for UTF-8 via the id enum
examples.description: Use `id::UTF8` (MIB 106) to reliably check the encoding without string comparisons.
examples.expectedOutput:
is UTF-8: true
UTF8 mib value: 106

examples.title: Object comparison and id enum
examples.description: Demonstrates `operator==` between two `text_encoding` objects, plus MIB comparison via the `id` enumeration.
examples.expectedOutput:
name: UTF-8
same object: true
mib: 106
id::UTF8: 106
is UTF-8 encoding: true

exercises.prompt: What will this program print on Godbolt (UTF-8 environment)?

options: The encoding of narrow string literals in the current translation unit

exercises.prompt: This code has a fragile encoding check that may give wrong results on some platforms. Find the buggy line.
exercises.explanation: Comparing `enc.name()` to the string `"UTF-8"` is fragile: UTF-8 has more than 10 registered aliases in the IANA registry (`"utf-8"`, `"UTF8"`, `"csUTF8"`, etc.). The `.name()` method returns only the canonical name for the current platform, which may differ. The reliable version: `enc.mib() == std::text_encoding::id::UTF8`.

options: `literal()` is the compile-time encoding of string literals; `environment()` is the runtime locale encoding
`environment()` is determined by the compiler; `literal()` by the OS
They always return the same value

questions.prompt: Which method reliably checks whether the literal encoding is UTF-8?
What does `std::text_encoding::literal().mib()` return on a UTF-8 platform?

challenge.title: The encoding_report() function
challenge.description: Write a function `encoding_report()` that prints: the name of the literal encoding, whether it is UTF-8, and its MIB number. Use `std::text_encoding::id::UTF8` for the check.
challenge.expectedOutput:
Encoding: UTF-8
Is UTF-8: true
MIB: 106
